items.front: When do the altitude and distance limitation tables apply?
items.back: When ATC specifies a route other than an established airway or route, for any portion of that route within controlled airspace.

items.front: What is the basic rule for nonestablished routes within controlled airspace?
items.back: Do not exceed the published altitude and distance limitations.

items.front: For an H-class VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN below 14,500 feet, what normal usable radius distance applies?
items.back: 40 miles.

items.front: For an H-class VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN between 18,000 and FL450, what normal usable radius distance applies?
items.back: 130 miles.

items.front: For an HH radio beacon, what usable radius distance applies for all altitudes?
items.back: 75 miles.

items.front: What are the normal ILS usable distances from the transmitter?
items.back: 10 miles for the glideslope and 18 miles for the localizer, unless current flight-check limitations differ.

items.front: What is the point of the 4-1-1 altitude/distance figures?
items.back: They show how to apply NAVAID usable-distance and altitude limits to each controlled-airspace segment of a non-airway route.

items.front: What is one main exception to the altitude and distance limitations?
items.back: Routing initiated by ATC or requested by the pilot when radar monitoring is provided.

items.front: Which GNSS suffix group is listed in the exception note when not on a random impromptu route?
items.back: /G, /L, /S, and /V.

items.front: What approval is required when operational necessity requires exceeding the limitations?
items.back: Approval from the Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Offices.

items.front: What routing is specifically listed as an exception?
items.back: Requested routing via an MTR.

items.front: What extra controller duty applies when clearing beyond the limitations?
items.back: Provide information and advice related to significant deviations from the expected flight path.

items.front: What note still applies to many RNAV aircraft on Q routes or random RNAV routes below FL450?
items.back: Radar separation is still required except for the listed GNSS-equipped aircraft and routes in the note.

items.front: When need altitude/distance limitations NOT be applied?
items.back: When routing is ATC-initiated or pilot-requested AND radar monitoring is provided. Also for GNSS-equipped aircraft /G /L /S /V not on random impromptu routes. Per 4-1-2(a).

items.front: What altitude is used when clearing an aircraft to cross or hold at a fix?
items.back: An altitude consistent with the limitations of the aid serving the fix.

items.front: Does paragraph 4-1-3 apply to both crossing and holding at a fix?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What should control of crossing or holding altitude never ignore?
items.back: The limitations of the NAVAID or aid used for the fix.

items.front: What is the core rule of paragraph 4-1-3?
items.back: Use an altitude consistent with the aid's limitations when clearing an aircraft to cross or hold at a fix.

items.front: When may a route not meeting service-volume limits be used under the VFR-on-top exception?
items.back: Only if the aircraft requests to operate VFR-on-top on that route.

items.front: How are TACAN-only aircraft expected to define routes between TACAN or VORTAC NAVAIDs?
items.back: In the same manner as VOR-equipped aircraft.

items.front: Outside Class A, what is a TACAN-only aircraft expected to request if insufficient TACAN or VORTAC NAVAIDs exist to define the route?
items.back: VFR-on-top.

items.front: What does paragraph 4-1-4 not allow the controller to do without a pilot request?
items.back: Use the VFR-on-top exception on a route that does not meet service-volume limitations.

items.front: What is the key condition of the VFR-on-top exception?
items.back: The pilot must request VFR-on-top on that route.

items.front: Over what fixes should aircraft position reports normally be requested?
items.back: Only over fixes shown on charts used for the altitude being flown, unless an exception applies.

items.front: Unless the pilot requests otherwise, what fixes are used for military turbojet single-piloted aircraft?
items.back: Only fixes shown on high altitude en route charts, high altitude instrument approach procedures charts, and SID charts.

items.front: What must be given to the pilot when using an unpublished fix?
items.back: The NAVAID name and, if appropriate, the radial, course, azimuth, and frequency or channel.

items.front: What is an unpublished fix?
items.back: One approved and planned for publication but not yet on charts, or one used under the listed criteria.

items.front: If an unpublished fix can be formed either by DME or an off-route radial, which is preferred?
items.back: DME, whenever possible.

items.front: When both NAVAIDs are within 30 NM of an unpublished fix, what minimum divergence angle applies?
items.back: 30 degrees.

items.front: Can a TACAN-only aircraft be held overhead a TACAN or VORTAC?
items.back: No.

items.front: Where may a DME fix not be established if inbound holding-course information would be derived from that NAVAID?
items.back: Within the NAVAID's no-course signal zone.

items.front: What item comes immediately after aircraft identification in the listed clearance-item order?
items.back: Clearance limit.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an airport, what must follow the airport name?
items.back: The word airport.

items.front: When the clearance limit is a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of NAVAID.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of intersection or waypoint.

items.front: What item is listed last in the clearance-item order?
items.back: Frequency and beacon code information.

items.front: With an altitude assignment to an airborne USAF aircraft, how many of the listed extra items may be included in the same transmission?
items.back: No more than one.

items.front: What is the standard order of IFR clearance items?
items.back:
1. Aircraft identification
2. Clearance limit (with 'airport' or NAVAID type)
3. SID or vectors
4. Route of flight
5. Altitude data in order flown
6. Mach number if applicable. Per 4-2-1(a-f).

items.front: Before switching an arriving IFR aircraft to CTAF at an airport without a tower or FSS, what must be provided if needed?
items.back: Instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan.

items.front: What is the cancellation instruction when the airport has an air/ground communications station?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR on (frequency).

items.front: What is one approved cancellation instruction when the airport has no air/ground communications station?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.

items.front: What is the other approved no-station cancellation instruction form?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with the serving FSS or ATC controlling facility.

items.front: How should ATC acknowledge receipt of the IFR cancellation?
items.back: IFR cancellation received.

items.front: In the no-station cancellation phrase, what word separates this frequency from the alternate reporting outlet?
items.back: Or.

items.front: At a no-station airport, must the pilot report cancellation only to flight service?
items.back: No. The pilot may be instructed to report it this frequency or with flight service or the named facility.

items.front: What should happen after a pilot reports IFR cancellation on frequency?
items.back: ATC should acknowledge the report.

items.front: How is a relayed clearance, advisory, or request prefaced through a non-ATC facility?
items.back: With the appropriate phrase such as ATC clears, ATC advises, or ATC requests.

items.front: Which organizations are specifically required to use the appropriate ATC prefix when relaying clearances?
items.back: Flight service stations and ARTCC Flight Data Units.

items.front: Does the prefix requirement apply only to relayed clearances?
items.back: No. It also applies to relayed information and requests for information.

items.front: What is one approved relay prefix?
items.back: ATC clears.

items.front: What should you do if specific clearance delivery instructions are appropriate?
items.back: Issue the specific clearance delivery instructions.

items.front: What circumstance triggers the rule in paragraph 4-2-3?
items.back: When specific clearance delivery instructions are appropriate.

items.front: What is the complete rule in paragraph 4-2-3?
items.back: Issue specific clearance delivery instructions, if appropriate.

items.front: What should not be done if needed delivery instructions are appropriate?
items.back: Do not omit them and expect the pilot to infer them.

items.front: How must a clearance be relayed?
items.back: Verbatim.

items.front: May a relayed clearance be paraphrased if the meaning seems unchanged?
items.back: No.

items.front: What is the full rule in paragraph 4-2-4?
items.back: Relay clearances verbatim.

items.front: What is not permitted when relaying a clearance?
items.back: Paraphrasing, summarizing, or substituting words.

items.front: How must clearances be relayed?
items.back: VERBATIM — do not paraphrase or summarize. Per 4-2-4.

items.front: What is one approved way to amend a previously issued route clearance?
items.back: State the route amendment and then say the rest of the route is unchanged.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to amend a route by identifying the portion being changed?
items.back: Change (portion of route) to read (new portion of route).

items.front: Does a direct clearance to a point on the previously issued route require the phrase rest of route unchanged?
items.back: No, though it may be used if needed to clarify where the route resumes.

items.front: When route or altitude in a previously issued clearance is amended, what must be restated?
items.back: All applicable altitude restrictions.

items.front: What happens if altitude to maintain is changed or restated and previously issued altitude restrictions are omitted?
items.back: Those previously issued altitude restrictions are canceled, including SID or STAR altitude restrictions if any.

items.front: Can ATC cancel or amend crossing altitudes and speed restrictions on obstacle departure procedures?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must be done if a speed restriction is declined because it cannot be complied with concurrently with a previously issued altitude restriction?
items.back: Issue an amended clearance.

items.front: What phrase is not a valid substitute for an amended clearance with altitude or speed restrictions?
items.back: Do the best you can.

items.front: What are the four methods for amending a route clearance?
items.back:
1. State which portion is amended and state amendment
2. State amendment then 'rest of route unchanged'
3. Issue 'cleared direct' to a point on previous route
4. Issue entire route stating the amendment. Per 4-2-5(a)(1-4).

items.front: What does a through clearance authorize?
items.back: It authorizes clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.

items.front: What is the basic phraseology for a through clearance?
items.back: Cleared through (airport) to (fix).

items.front: In through-clearance phraseology, what follows the word through?
items.back: The intermediate-stop airport.

items.front: In through-clearance phraseology, what follows the word to?
items.back: The final fix.

items.front: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?
items.back: Via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan.

items.front: When is the ALTRV phrase inserted into the clearance?
items.back: When the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.

items.front: What phases does normal ALTRV coverage usually include?
items.back: Departure, climb, cruise, and arrival.

items.front: Normal ALTRV coverage usually extends to what point?
items.back: To the holding pattern or the point or time ATC provides separation.

items.front: Does normal ALTRV coverage end automatically at top of climb?
items.back: No.

items.front: What words surround the mission name in the ALTRV phrase?
items.back: Altitude reservation before it, and flight plan after it.

items.front: If a flight will operate IFR first and then VFR, where is it cleared?
items.back: To the fix where the IFR part of the flight ends.

items.front: How is a VFR-then-IFR flight treated at departure?
items.back: As a VFR departure.

items.front: When is the IFR clearance issued to a VFR-then-IFR flight?
items.back: When the aircraft requests it approaching the fix where IFR begins.

items.front: What code should be assigned to a Mode C aircraft changing from VFR to IFR?
items.back: A beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

items.front: What must ATC ask a below-minimum-altitude pilot requesting IFR?
items.back: Whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.

items.front: If the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the proper minimum altitude, what may ATC issue?
items.back: The IFR clearance.

items.front: If the pilot cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the proper minimum altitude, what should ATC instruct?
items.back: Maintain VFR and state intentions.

items.front: Who is responsible for terrain and obstruction clearance until the appropriate minimum altitude is reached in the pop-up IFR case?
items.back: The pilot.

items.front: What must ATC not assign or imply below the MIA or MEA in this pop-up IFR case?
items.back: Specific course guidance.

items.front: What must you ask a VFR aircraft requesting IFR below minimum IFR altitude?
items.back: Are you able to maintain your own terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching (appropriate MVA/MIA/MEA/OROCA)? If unable, instruct to maintain VFR. Per 4-2-8(d)(1-3).

items.front: What must be verified first before processing an airfile request unless other coordination exists?
items.back: That the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction.

items.front: After jurisdiction is confirmed, what information should be obtained from an airfile aircraft?
items.back: The information needed to provide IFR service.

items.front: What are the three basic airfile outcomes listed in 4-2-9?
items.back: Issue a destination clearance, issue a short-range clearance, or instruct the aircraft to contact flight service if the flight plan cannot be processed.

items.front: What phrase is used if the airfile aircraft is cleared to the destination airport?
items.back: Cleared to (destination) airport.

items.front: If a clearance is to a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of NAVAID.

items.front: If a clearance is to an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of intersection or waypoint.

items.front: Do the airfile procedures imply that airfile processing has priority over another ATC duty?
items.back: No.

items.front: When may the word takeoff be used in ATC phraseology?
items.back: Only to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance.

items.front: What words should replace takeoff in other departure instructions?
items.back: Words such as depart, departure, or fly.

items.front: Is takeoff appropriate in a heading instruction such as runway heading?
items.back: No.

items.front: May takeoff be used when canceling a takeoff clearance?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What simple substitute fits a route or heading instruction instead of takeoff?
items.back: Fly.

items.front: When should the term 'takeoff' be used?
items.back: ONLY to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance. Use 'depart,' 'departure,' or 'fly' in all other contexts. Per 4-3-1.

items.front: Unless alternate procedures apply, where must terminal departure times be forwarded?
items.back: To the facility from which the clearance was received and to the terminal departure controller when that position is involved.

items.front: What is one explicit exception to the normal forwarding requirement in paragraph 4-3-10?
items.back: Automatic departure messages are being transmitted between automated facilities.

items.front: What does a flashing DM in the data block signify?
items.back: Unsuccessful transmission of a departure message.

items.front: What LOA procedures are named as alternatives for equivalent handling in the note to paragraph 4-3-10?
items.back: Assumed departure times or mandatory radar handoff procedures.

items.front: When must the terminal departure controller receive the forwarded departure time?
items.back: When that position is involved in the departure sequence.

items.front: What must always be included when a departure clearance is relayed through an FSS or dispatcher?
items.back: The airport of departure.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an airport, what must follow the airport name?
items.back: The word airport.

items.front: When the clearance limit is a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of NAVAID.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of intersection or waypoint.

items.front: How is the destination handled for Air Force One operations?
items.back: Do not specify the destination airport; use destination as filed.

items.front: What happens if an aircraft is vectored off a published SID or ODP?
items.back: The DP is canceled and ATC becomes responsible for separation from terrain and obstructions.

items.front: When a location without ATC service is within a Class E surface area and a turn or heading is needed after departure, what must be obtained first?
items.back: The pilot's concurrence.

items.front: At all other airports, what should not be specified after takeoff?
items.back: Direction of takeoff or turn after takeoff.

items.front: What phrase is used when a SID contains published crossing restrictions and an interim altitude must be issued?
items.back: Climb via SID except maintain (altitude).

items.front: What route condition must be met before an abbreviated departure clearance can be used?
items.back: The filed route must not have been changed before departure.

items.front: What must all ATC facilities concerned have for an abbreviated departure clearance to work?
items.back: Sufficient route-of-flight information to exercise their control responsibilities.

items.front: Before an IFR flight departs under abbreviated procedures, what information must be relayed between the facilities concerned?
items.back: Destination airport information.

items.front: What altitude item must still be stated in an abbreviated departure clearance?
items.back: The assigned altitude.

items.front: What does FRC mean?
items.back: Full Route Clearance Necessary.

items.front: If FRC followed by a fix appears, how far must the full route clearance be issued?
items.back: To the specified fix.

items.front: What is the core no-change abbreviated phrase with a SID?
items.back: Cleared to (destination) airport, (SID) departure, then as filed.

items.front: When should abbreviated departure clearance procedures not be used?
items.back: When the pilot requests a detailed clearance or the operation requires special military handling such as ALTRV.

items.front: What release control must an untowered IFR departure receive?
items.back: A departure release, a hold for release, or a release time.

items.front: What does hold for release mean?
items.back: The departure clearance is not valid until additional instructions are received.

items.front: What does a release time specify?
items.back: The earliest time an aircraft may depart.

items.front: When issuing a release time using a specified clock time, what else must be given?
items.back: A time check.

items.front: Does a release time using a specified number of minutes require a time check?
items.back: No.

items.front: When CFR is in effect, what is the normal airborne window around the assigned time?
items.back: From 2 minutes before to 1 minute after the assigned time, unless otherwise coordinated.

items.front: What extra instruction must accompany a clearance void time at an airport without an operating control tower?
items.back: Require the pilot to advise ATC of intentions no later than 30 minutes after the void time if not airborne.

items.front: What does an expired clearance void time do?
items.back: It withdraws authority to depart IFR until a new release is issued and acknowledged; it does not cancel the departure clearance or IFR flight plan.

items.front: What are departure release, hold for release, and clearance void times?
items.back: Release: aircraft cleared for departure. Hold for release: clearance not valid until further instructions. Void time: if not off by specified time, clearance is void and pilot must advise intentions within 30 minutes. Per 4-3-4(a-f).

items.front: What approval is required before releasing an aircraft affected by a ground stop?
items.back: Approval from the originator of the ground stop.

items.front: May the aircraft be released just because the receiving facility can accept it?
items.back: No.

items.front: Who controls any exception to the ground stop for that aircraft?
items.back: The originator of the ground stop.

items.front: What is the default action when the ground stop remains active and no exception has been approved?
items.back: Keep the aircraft on the ground.

items.front: Can an aircraft be released during a ground stop?
items.back: No — do not release if a ground stop applicable to that aircraft is in effect, without approval of the GS originator. Per 4-3-5.

items.front: When aircraft choose to take delay on the ground before departure, what is the normal sequencing basis?
items.back: The order in which the requests for clearance were originally made, if practicable.

items.front: What qualifier applies to preserving the original request order in ground-delay sequencing?
items.back: If practicable.

items.front: Does paragraph 4-3-6 address delay taken on the ground or in holding after departure?
items.back: On the ground before departure.

items.front: What should not normally displace the first requester in ground-delay sequencing?
items.back: A later requester simply being faster or more convenient to launch.

items.front: In what order are clearances issued during ground delay?
items.back: In the order requests were ORIGINALLY made, if practicable. Per 4-3-6.

items.front: Who must be informed of anticipated departure delays?
items.back: Approach control facilities and/or towers.

items.front: What kind of delays are covered in paragraph 4-3-7?
items.back: Anticipated departure delays.

items.front: Is a tower one of the explicit coordination targets in paragraph 4-3-7?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What is the basic controller action in paragraph 4-3-7?
items.back: Inform the affected approach control facilities and/or towers.

items.front: What is the baseline predeparture coordination trigger in paragraph 4-3-8?
items.back: The departure point is less than 15 minutes flying time from the transferring facility's boundary.

items.front: When automatic transfer of data between automated systems will occur, to what threshold may the flying-time requirement be reduced?
items.back: 5 minutes.

items.front: What can replace the flying-time trigger when automatic data transfer will occur and both facilities agree?
items.back: A mileage-from-boundary parameter.

items.front: What kind of agreements are encouraged when facilities need earlier coordination?
items.back: Agreements requiring additional time.

items.front: If agreements establish mandatory radar handoff procedures, when must coordination be effected?
items.back: In a timely manner prior to transfer of control.

items.front: Does mandatory radar handoff automatically eliminate coordination?
items.back: No.

items.front: When must departure coordination occur with receiving facility?
items.back: When departure point is less than 15 minutes flying time from boundary. With automation: 5 minutes or mileage parameter. Per 4-3-8(a).

items.front: After any necessary approval, what may ATC authorize for an aircraft that filed IFR but requests to depart VFR?
items.back: A VFR departure with instructions on the proper frequency and, if appropriate, when or where to contact for the IFR clearance.

items.front: What phrase begins the VFR release of an IFR departure?
items.back: VFR departure authorized.

items.front: What should be suggested if the facility responsible for the IFR clearance cannot issue it?
items.back: That the delay be taken on the ground.

items.front: If the pilot insists on departing VFR and obtaining the IFR clearance in the air, who must be informed?
items.back: The facility or sector holding the flight plan.

items.front: If possible, what additional detail should be given to the facility or sector holding the flight plan when the pilot insists on a VFR departure?
items.back: The VFR departure time.

items.front: Which facilities are named in paragraph 4-3-9 as places where this VFR release request may be handled?
items.back: A terminal facility, FSS, ARTCC Flight Data Unit, or air/ground communications station.

items.front: Does paragraph 4-3-9 apply to an aircraft that has filed an IFR flight plan?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What principle governs route use here?
items.back: Clear aircraft via routes consistent with the altitude stratum in which the operation will be conducted.

items.front: When a GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on a random RNAV route is not being radar monitored, what kind of route must it be on?
items.back: A point-to-point route.

items.front: What kinds of points may define that point-to-point route?
items.back: Published NAVAIDs, waypoints, fixes, or airports recallable from the aircraft navigation database and displayed on controller maps or charts.

items.front: When applying nonradar separation on a random RNAV route, what is the maximum distance between points?
items.back: 500 miles.

items.front: How much protected airspace is required either side of the route centerline on that random RNAV segment?
items.back: 4 miles.

items.front: What altitude condition applies to assigned altitudes on that projected route segment?
items.back: They must be at or above the highest MIA along the projected route segment, including its protected airspace.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a random impromptu route?
items.back: Direct (name of NAVAID/waypoint/fix/airport).

items.front: What phraseology is used for a point-to-point segment beginning after a fix?
items.back: After (fix) proceed direct (fix).

items.front: What phraseology may be used to clear an aircraft within a quadrant of a NAVAID?
items.back: Cleared to fly (specified) quadrant of (NAVAID name and type) within (number of miles) mile radius.

items.front: What additional service is required when RNAV aircraft transition between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes?
items.back: Radar monitoring.

items.front: What is one approved route-structure transition method here?
items.back: Assign a SID or STAR.

items.front: May route-structure transitions be made by vectoring aircraft to or from radials, courses, or azimuths of the ATS route assigned?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: May departing or arriving aircraft be cleared directly to or between the NAVAIDs forming the ATS route assigned?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: For the random RNAV transition method, what kinds of points may be used on the new route?
items.back: A NAVAID, waypoint, airport, or fix on the new route.

items.front: Below FL180, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes on a military route segment?
items.back: 80 miles.

items.front: At FL180 and above, what is the maximum distance allowed between degree-distance fixes?
items.back: 260 miles.

items.front: What three elements compose a degree-distance fix?
items.back: A location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in miles from the NAVAID used.

items.front: How are degree-distance fixes used to define a route treated for reporting purposes unless ATC authorizes otherwise?
items.back: As compulsory reporting points.

items.front: What is the sample degree-distance fix given in the paragraph?
What NAVAID-use restriction applies to degree-distance fixes?
items.back: The selected NAVAIDs must be authorized for use at the altitude being flown and within their published service volume area.

items.front: When a depicted alternative route is issued to a non-RNAV aircraft because part of a route is unusable, what word must immediately precede that route?
items.back: Substitute.

items.front: What is one approved non-RNAV alternative route type when part of an airway or route is unusable because of NAVAID status?
items.back: A route defined by NAVAID radials, courses, or azimuths.

items.front: What is one other approved non-RNAV alternative route type here?
items.back: A route defined as direct to or between NAVAIDs.

items.front: What is one more approved non-RNAV alternative route type here?
items.back: Vectors.

items.front: What must RNAV aircraft be told if they will proceed to the location of the unusable NAVAID?
items.back: Inform them of the NAVAID outage.

items.front: What word precedes an alternative route description?
items.back: 'Substitute' — immediately preceding the alternative route. Per 4-4-4(a).

items.front: When may routes through Class G airspace be included in a clearance?
items.back: Only when requested by the pilot.

items.front: What filed route is considered a pilot request for routing through Class G airspace?
items.back: A random RNAV route filed through Class G airspace.

items.front: What filed route is also considered a pilot request for routing through Class G airspace?
items.back: A flight plan containing MTR segments in or through Class G airspace.

items.front: Are separation criteria applicable in Class G airspace?
items.back: No.

items.front: What ATC services are still applicable in Class G airspace to aircraft in direct communication with ATC?
items.back: Traffic advisories and safety alerts.

items.front: En route, when may you issue a revised routing clearance that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in your facility's airspace?
items.back: Only when requested by the pilot or when operational necessity dictates.

items.front: What two conditions trigger the basic restriction against taking an aircraft off its route unless operational necessity dictates?
items.back: The aircraft is part of a known traffic management initiative, or the route under consideration is within a protected segment.

items.front: If a protected-segment routing amendment is made by an ATCS, with whom must coordination occur?
items.back: TMU.

items.front: If a protected-segment routing amendment is made by a terminal facility TMU, with whom must coordination occur?
items.back: The overlying ARTCC TMU.

items.front: If a protected-segment routing amendment is outside the ARTCC TMU's own facility, with whom must coordination occur?
items.back: ATCSCC.

items.front: What does the note say this paragraph must not preclude?
items.back: Routing clearances that conform to an LOA or SOP, or are required to maintain separation or comply with traffic flow management initiatives.

items.front: What vertical separation normally applies up to and including FL410?
items.back: 1,000 feet.

items.front: What basic vertical separation applies above FL410 unless an exception applies?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: At or above FL290, what separation applies between a non-RVSM aircraft and any other aircraft at or above FL290?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: In oceanic airspace above FL450, what separation applies between a supersonic aircraft and any other aircraft?
items.back: 4,000 feet.

items.front: Above FL600 between military aircraft, what vertical separation applies?
items.back: 5,000 feet.

items.front: What does the non-RVSM exception change in the normal vertical-separation rule?
items.back: At or above FL290, once a non-RVSM aircraft is involved, 2,000 feet must be applied from all other aircraft at or above FL290.

items.front: At and below FL410, what altitude pattern applies on courses from 0 through 179 degrees?
items.back: Odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.

items.front: At and below FL410, what altitude pattern applies on courses from 180 through 359 degrees?
items.back: Even cardinal altitudes or flight levels at 2,000-foot intervals.

items.front: Below 3,000 feet above the surface, what flight-direction rule applies?
items.back: Any course may be assigned any altitude.

items.front: Above FL410, what eastbound pattern applies?
items.back: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL450.

items.front: Above FL410, what westbound pattern applies?
items.back: Odd cardinal flight levels at 4,000-foot intervals beginning with FL430.

items.front: On one-way routes above FL410, what kind of flight level may be assigned?
items.back: Any odd cardinal flight level above FL410.

items.front: What is the IFR cruising altitude rule?
items.back: Eastbound (0-179 degrees magnetic): ODD thousands. Westbound (180-359 degrees): EVEN thousands. Per 4-5-2.

items.front: When traffic conditions justify a random altitude beyond your facility, what must exist?
items.back: A letter of agreement covering the specific operations and procedures.

items.front: When traffic conditions justify a random altitude that remains within your facility, what approval is needed?
items.back: Prior approval from affected positions or sectors, unless covered by a facility directive.

items.front: When weather justifies the exception, what approvals are needed?
items.back: Prior approval from affected positions or sectors and, if necessary, from the adjacent facility concerned.

items.front: When aircraft operational limitations justify the exception, what extra condition must exist besides coordination?
items.back: The pilot must inform ATC that the available appropriate altitude exceeds the aircraft's operational limitations.

items.front: When do mission requirements justify a random altitude under this paragraph?
items.back: Only when the aircraft is operating on an MTR.

items.front: What special case applies to military aircraft on random routes?
items.back: They may use the exception when prior approval is obtained from the facility concerned.

items.front: When do you use the lowest-usable-flight-level table?
items.back: When a pressure change affects a usable flight level in your area and you must clear aircraft at or above 18,000 feet MSL.

items.front: What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting of 29.92 or higher?
What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting from 29.91 through 28.92?
What is the lowest usable flight level with an altimeter setting from 28.91 through 27.92?
What determines the lowest usable flight level?
items.back: Altimeter setting and terrain considerations. Per 4-5-4.

items.front: When is an adjusted minimum flight level required?
items.back: When the minimum IFR altitude is at or above 18,000 feet MSL and the altimeter setting is below 29.92.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 29.91 through 29.42?
items.back: 500 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 29.41 through 28.92?
items.back: 1,000 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 28.91 through 28.42?
items.back: 1,500 feet.

items.front: What adjustment applies with an altimeter setting from 28.41 through 27.92?
items.back: 2,000 feet.

items.front: What is the basic altitude rule when an MEA exists on the route segment being flown?
items.back: Assign at or above the MEA unless one of the paragraph's specific exceptions applies.

items.front: When may an aircraft be cleared below the MEA but not below the MOCA on the route segment being flown?
items.back: When it remains at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace and one of the listed conditions is met.

items.front: What distance limit applies to the VOR/VORTAC/TACAN below-MEA option?
items.back: It applies only within 22 miles of that NAVAID.

items.front: What must ATC do if radar procedures are used below the MEA and no MOCA is published?
items.back: Assign at or above the MVA or MIA along the route and issue lost communications instructions.

items.front: What equipment alone can satisfy one of the below-MEA exceptions?
items.back: GNSS equipment.

items.front: When may an aircraft be cleared on a jet route below the MEA or above the maximum authorized altitude?
items.back: When radar service is provided and the clearance does not go below the prescribed minimum IFR altitude.

items.front: If a higher MEA begins at a fix and no MCA is specified, when should the climb begin?
items.back: Prior to or immediately after passing the fix where the higher MEA is designated.

items.front: If an MCA is specified, what must ATC ensure?
items.back: The climb begins before the fix so the aircraft crosses the fix at or above the MCA.

items.front: When issuing cruise with an airport clearance limit and an unpublished route, what is normally required?
items.back: An appropriate crossing altitude that ensures IFR obstruction clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where altitude information is available.

items.front: When may a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach be issued without a crossing restriction?
items.back: When the airport has no published instrument approach procedure.

items.front: When does a climb or descent restriction require a UTC time check?
items.back: When the restriction uses a clock time, except when the clearance is issued by CPDLC or by elapsed time in radar contact with direct two-way VHF/UHF voice communication.

items.front: What does a crossing altitude do to a descent at pilot's discretion?
items.back: It authorizes descent at pilot's discretion for the portion of flight to which the crossing restriction applies.

items.front: When canceling a pilot's-discretion portion by assigning a new altitude, what must the controller say?
items.back: Identify the new altitude as an amended altitude.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a basic crossing restriction at or above an altitude?
items.back: Cross (fix, waypoint) at or above (altitude).

items.front: What phraseology is used for direct-to plus descend-via when the direct fix has no published altitude?
items.back: Proceed direct (fix or waypoint), cross (fix or waypoint) at (altitude), then descend via (STAR name and number).

items.front: After an interim altitude on a SID, what instruction is used when ATC can clear the aircraft back to the published top altitude?
items.back: Climb via SID.

items.front: If ATC needs a different top altitude while retaining published restrictions on the SID, what phraseology is used?
items.back: Climb via SID except maintain (altitude).

items.front: What phraseology is used when a climb-via or descend-via clearance must be restated after clearing the aircraft direct to a waypoint or fix?
items.back: Cleared or proceed direct (waypoint or fix), then climb via SID, or the equivalent descend-via restatement.

items.front: If practicable, what should be told to an aircraft when a climb or descent cannot yet be issued?
items.back: When to expect higher or lower, or when to request the altitude change from another facility.

items.front: What phraseology is used to advise an aircraft of an expected climb or descent in miles or minutes?
items.back: Expect higher or lower in (number of miles or minutes) miles or minutes.

items.front: What alternate advisory may be used instead of an expect-higher or expect-lower message?
items.back: Request altitude or flight-level change from (facility) at the specified time, fix, or altitude.

items.front: Why is this paragraph operationally useful?
items.back: It lets the pilot plan ahead for a climb, descent, or handoff-driven altitude request when the clearance cannot be issued immediately.

items.front: When should altitude confirmation normally be requested in nonradar operations?
items.back: On initial contact and when position reports are received, unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.front: What does initial contact mean here?
items.back: The pilot's first radio contact with each sector or position.

items.front: What is one exception to the initial-contact altitude-verification requirement?
items.back: A terminal aircraft transferred from another sector or position within the same facility.

items.front: What must controllers in the USA do with all pilot altitude readbacks?
items.back: Reconfirm them.

items.front: What phraseology is used when verifying a flight level at or above the lowest usable flight level?
items.back: Verify assigned flight level (flight level).

items.front: What phraseology is used when a correct altitude readback is reconfirmed?
items.back: Affirmative (altitude).

items.front: If the route beyond a clearance limit differs from the last routing cleared, what must be issued?
items.back: The route the pilot can expect beyond the clearance limit.

items.front: When may holding instructions be eliminated in a clearance to a holding fix?
items.back: When the pilot is informed that no delay is expected.

items.front: When may most holding instructions be omitted for a charted pattern already in the assigned procedure or route?
items.back: When the charted holding direction and the words "as published" are still stated, unless the pilot requests complete holding instructions.

items.front: When is the EFC item omitted?
items.back: When no delay is expected.

items.front: When delay is expected, when should the clearance-limit, holding, and EFC items normally be issued?
items.back: At least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit, or immediately if it is already less than 5 minutes from the fix.

items.front: What phraseology is used when no delay is expected at the fix?
items.back: Cleared to (fix), no delay expected.

items.front: What phraseology is used when onward routing beyond the clearance limit must be stated later?
items.back: Expect further clearance via (routing).

items.front: What phraseology is used when an accurate delay estimate cannot immediately be determined?
items.back: Delay indefinite, (reason if known), expect further clearance (time).

items.front: If no delay is expected, when should a clearance beyond the clearance limit be issued?
items.back: As soon as possible and, whenever possible, at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix.

items.front: What three items are included when issuing a clearance beyond a clearance limit?
items.back: The new clearance limit or approach clearance, the route of flight, and the assigned altitude if it differs from the present altitude.

items.front: When may the phrase "via last routing cleared" be used?
items.back: Only when the most recently issued routing to the new clearance limit remains valid and verbiage will be reduced.

items.front: If no holding pattern is charted and none was issued, what should pilots do before reaching the fix?
items.back: Ask ATC for holding instructions prior to reaching the fix.

items.front: If a pilot cannot obtain holding instructions before reaching an uncharted fix, what hold is expected?
items.back: A standard pattern on the course on which the aircraft approached the fix, while requesting further clearance as soon as possible.

items.front: When must a clearance beyond the limit be issued?
items.back: If no delay is expected, as soon as possible and at least 5 minutes before the aircraft reaches the fix whenever possible. Per 4-6-2(a).

items.front: What should you do as soon as possible when you delay or expect to delay aircraft?
items.back: Advise your supervisor or flow controller.

items.front: When arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, when must the responsible center issue total delay information?
items.back: As soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area, and whenever possible by the first center controller to communicate with the aircraft.

items.front: When tower en route control service is being provided, which facility issues total delay information after the aircraft enters its area?
items.back: The approach control facility whose area contains the destination airport.

items.front: When may the 30-minute delay advisories be omitted?
items.back: When total delay information is available via ATIS and the pilot has not requested delay information.

items.front: What phraseology is used for total arrival delay information?
items.back: (Airport) arrival delays (time in minutes or hours).

items.front: What direction information must a holding clearance include?
items.back: The direction of holding from the fix or waypoint.

items.front: When may the holding fix be omitted from the holding-instruction element?
items.back: When it was already included at the beginning of the transmission as the clearance limit.

items.front: How is leg length specified when DME or RNAV is used?
items.back: In miles, unless the pilot requests minutes or the controller considers minutes necessary.

items.front: When must turn direction be stated in holding instructions?
items.back: When left turns are to be made, when the pilot requests it, or when the controller considers it necessary.

items.front: When is a maximum holding airspeed advisory required?
items.back: When the pattern protects a higher approved speed, when the aircraft is observed deviating from holding airspace, or when the airspeed-restricted holding icon has not been published.

items.front: What phraseology states the protected holding speed?
items.back: Maximum holding airspeed is (speed) knots.

items.front: What phraseology begins a standard holding instruction?
items.back: Hold (direction) of (fix or waypoint) on (specified radial, course, bearing, track, airway, azimuth, or route).

items.front: When may a visual holding point be used?
items.back: When the pilot can determine it by visual reference to the surface and is familiar with it.

items.front: What phraseology is used for a visual holding point?
items.back: Hold at (location) until (time or other condition).

items.front: What if the pilot is not familiar with the proposed visual holding point?
items.back: It may not be used.

items.front: What two conditions must both exist before a visual holding point is acceptable?
items.back: The pilot must be able to determine it by visual reference to the surface and must be familiar with it.

items.front: When may a pilot's requested deviation from the prescribed holding flight path be approved?
items.back: When obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

items.front: What two factors control approval of a holding flight-path deviation?
items.back: Obstacles and traffic conditions.

items.front: If traffic conditions do not permit the requested holding deviation, what is the correct action?
items.back: Do not approve the deviation.

items.front: Does pilot request alone justify a holding flight-path deviation?
items.back: No. Obstacles and traffic conditions must also permit it.

items.front: How is protection defined for an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Separate it from any aircraft occupying the course it would follow if it stopped receiving the NAVAID signal.

items.front: What traffic matters when protecting an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Traffic occupying the course the holding aircraft would follow after signal loss.

items.front: Why is extra protection required at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Because the aircraft may continue along a predictable course if it loses the NAVAID signal while holding.

items.front: What if another aircraft is about to enter that protected signal-loss course?
items.back: Provide separation so the two aircraft do not occupy that course together.

items.front: What weather condition triggers the ILS holding restriction?
items.back: An official ceiling below 800 feet or visibility of 2 miles.

items.front: What holding position is prohibited once the ILS protection trigger exists?
items.back: Holding below 5,000 feet AGL inbound toward the airport on or within 1 statute mile of the localizer between the OM or substitute fix and the airport.

items.front: What additional limitation appears in the USAF note?
items.back: The holding restriction applies only when an arriving aircraft is between the OM or substitute fix and the runway.

items.front: What distance from the localizer is part of the protected area?
items.back: On or within 1 statute mile of the localizer.

items.front: What altitude floor is part of the prohibited hold area once the trigger exists?
items.back: Below 5,000 feet AGL.

items.front: What must an arrival clearance specify as the clearance limit?
items.back: The destination airport, a NAVAID, or an intersection or waypoint, as appropriate.

items.front: When a STAR or RNAV STAR is assigned, what must the clearance include?
items.back: The STAR name and the transition, if necessary.

items.front: If a STAR transition serves multiple runways, when must the intended runway be stated?
items.back: On initial contact or as soon as practical.

items.front: If the runway assignment or runway change is not issued before 10 NM from the runway-transition waypoint, what must be provided?
items.back: Radar vectors to final.

items.front: What altitude information may be part of an arrival clearance?
items.back: An assigned altitude or instructions to vertically navigate on the STAR or STAR transition.

items.front: What phraseology states a STAR with transition?
items.back: (STAR name and number) arrival, (transition name) transition.

items.front: What phraseology is used to change a runway transition and give vectors to final?
items.back: Change or amend transition to (runway), turn left or right or heading (heading) for vector to final approach course.

items.front: When is approach information provided by en route and terminal approach control sectors?
items.back: On initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter.

items.front: When may ATIS approach information be omitted?
items.back: When the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: At an airport without ATIS, after the pilot advises receipt of automated weather, which items may be omitted?
items.back: Surface wind, threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility, and altimeter setting.

items.front: What weather-related items are part of the normal approach-information package when they are not omitted?
items.back: Surface wind, threshold-triggered ceiling and visibility, and the altimeter setting.

items.front: What must controllers provide upon pilot request regarding automated weather?
items.back: The frequency where automated weather may be obtained and, if appropriate, that airport weather is not available.

items.front: If the pilot cannot receive the ASOS or AWOS broadcast, what must ATC issue?
items.back: The last long-line disseminated weather.

items.front: What phraseology is used for the automated weather frequency advisory?
items.back: (Airport) AWOS/ASOS weather available on (frequency).

items.front: When need special weather observations no longer be issued separately?
items.back: After they are included in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: When tower and TRACON are part of the same facility, what arrival information is forwarded to the tower?
items.back: Aircraft identification, type if needed for separation, and the approach procedure and or runway if different from that in use.

items.front: What is one item approach control forwards to centers about holding operations?
items.back: The highest altitude being used by approach control at the holding fix.

items.front: When must a revised EFC be forwarded to center?
items.back: When it differs by 10 minutes or more from the one issued by center.

items.front: When may STARS modify or quick look functions be used to forward arrival data?
items.back: When the tower has STARS data on its CTRD and a facility directive or letter of agreement outlines the transfer procedures.

items.front: Who determines whether conditions are adequate for using STARS arrival data on the CTRD where authorized?
items.back: The local controller.

items.front: Before issuing an approach clearance, what destination information must be provided when known?
items.back: Abnormal operation of approach or landing aids and destination airport conditions that might restrict an approach or landing.

items.front: When may that airport-condition information be omitted?
items.back: When it is contained in ATIS and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.

items.front: Which en route airport-condition items are specifically excluded from the package?
items.back: Taxiway, apron, or service-area NOTAMs outside the landing area, plus other listed exclusions such as obstruction information for instrument approaches and cold-temperature compensation information.

items.front: When may RwyCC from a FICON NOTAM be issued?
items.back: Before or with approach clearance, before en route descent clearance, prior to departure in terminal operations, and as soon as possible after later changes.

items.front: May RwyCC be issued instead of the full FICON NOTAM?
items.back: Yes. The full FICON NOTAM is issued on pilot request if workload permits.

items.front: Does a pilot saying, "We have the NOTAMs," relieve ATC of the duty to issue restricting airport conditions?
items.back: No.

items.front: When must facilities coordinate during an ILS runway switch at an airport with multiple systems not used simultaneously?
items.back: When changing from one ILS to another under those conditions.

items.front: With whom must the ILS switch be coordinated?
items.back: Facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the affected NAVAIDs.

items.front: Why is this coordination required?
items.back: Because other facilities are using fixes formed by reference to the NAVAIDs being switched.

items.front: When should the coordination occur relative to the switch?
items.back: Before making the switch.

items.front: Before issuing a lower altitude under advance descent clearance near an adjacent center boundary, what must be done?
items.back: Coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude.

items.front: When should advance descent action be started?
items.back: At a distance sufficient to allow normal descent and speed reduction.

items.front: Which aircraft does this rule cover?
items.back: Aircraft landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary.

items.front: Why is the action started early?
items.back: To permit normal descent and speed reduction before arrival.

items.front: When are advance descent clearances issued?
items.back: When it will provide operational advantage. Per 4-7-2.

items.front: Under an authorized SFA procedure, when is a frequency change normally avoided?
items.back: After the aircraft begins approach or after initial contact during an en route descent, until a landing or low approach has been completed.

items.front: What type of aircraft does this SFA rule address?
items.back: Military single-piloted turbojet aircraft on an IFR flight plan under a letter-of-agreement SFA procedure.

items.front: Name one listed exception to the SFA no-frequency-change rule.
items.back: Daylight VFR conditions, pilot request, IFR cancellation, an emergency, or a visual approach.

items.front: If the pilot cancels IFR during SFA, may a frequency change be required?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What is a Single Frequency Approach (SFA)?
items.back: An approach using a single frequency for both communication and navigation. Per 4-7-3.

items.front: For military single-piloted turbojet aircraft in IFR conditions or at night, what is the general rule on frequency and beacon changes?
items.back: Avoid them to the maximum extent communications capabilities and traffic will permit.

items.front: If a frequency or beacon change is required, when should the instruction be given?
items.back: Early enough for the change to be completed before the approach fix or handoff point.

items.front: What should be minimized below 2,500 feet above the surface?
items.back: Frequency and radar beacon changes.

items.front: During what aircraft maneuver should frequency and beacon changes be avoided?
items.back: While the aircraft is making a turn.

items.front: When may a change to tower frequency be allowed on a nonradar approach?
items.back: When one of the listed conditions exists, such as at least 1,500-foot ceiling and 5-mile visibility, pilot visual reference to the surface, contact approach, or visual approach.

items.front: After a missed approach, when must a frequency or beacon change not be required?
items.back: Before the aircraft reaches the missed approach altitude, the MEA, or the MVA.

items.front: When may a military turbojet en route descent be used in a nonradar environment?
items.back: When radar capability exists for vectors to final on a published high-altitude, PAR, or ASR approach and other than normal vectoring delays are not anticipated.

items.front: Before descent below the highest initial-approach-fix altitude, what must the aircraft be told?
items.back: The approach to expect and that radar vectors will be provided to final approach course.

items.front: When must current weather be included in the military turbojet descent briefing?
items.back: When the ceiling is below 1,000 feet, or USAF 1,500 feet, or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater, or visibility is under 3 miles.

items.front: If the pilot has the current ATIS before the descent briefing, what may be omitted?
items.back: Any required briefing items already contained in the ATIS broadcast.

items.front: How is the military turbojet descent point determined?
items.back: Add 10 to the first two digits of the flight level.

items.front: For FL370, what descent-point distance does the rule produce?
items.back: 47 miles.

items.front: May ATC terminate the en route descent without pilot consent in normal operations?
items.back: No, except when required by radar outage or an emergency.

items.front: What information is forwarded to a nonapproach control tower or, in Alaska, to FSS for arriving aircraft?
items.back: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and the type of approach or equivalent arrival details.

items.front: Before transfer of jurisdiction to approach control, what information is forwarded?
items.back: Aircraft identification, type and equipment suffix, ETA or actual time and altitude over the clearance limit, plus other required limit and control-transfer details.

items.front: When may the ETA be omitted in forwarded arrival information to approach control?
items.back: When the information is being forwarded during a radar handoff.

items.front: If the clearance limit is not the destination airport, what must usually be forwarded?
items.back: The clearance limit and any EFC issued.

items.front: What phraseology acknowledges transfer of control responsibility to approach control?
items.back: Your control, or your control at (time, fix, or altitude).

items.front: When must en route weather be transmitted to an arriving aircraft before or as part of the approach clearance?
items.back: When official weather is below the stated ceiling or visibility thresholds and the report is being delivered directly or through an authorized relay path.

items.front: What relay method is authorized when the weather report is not transmitted directly to the pilot?
items.back: A communications station other than an air carrier company radio, or a nonapproach control facility directed to issue current weather.

items.front: May a nonapproach control facility be instructed to issue current weather to satisfy this rule?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What kind of weather changes are also transmitted under this rule?
items.back: Changes classified as special weather observations.

items.front: When must weather be transmitted to arriving aircraft?
items.back: When official weather shows ceiling below 1,000 feet (USAF: 1,500 feet) or below highest circling minimum (whichever higher), or visibility less than 3 miles. Per 4-7-7.

items.front: If an arriving aircraft reports weather below the pilot's landing minima, what should ATC do for that aircraft?
items.back: Issue appropriate instructions to hold or proceed to another airport.

items.front: What must ATC do with other aircraft desiring approaches after a below-minima report?
items.back: Adjust their position in the landing sequence as necessary and issue approach clearances accordingly.

items.front: Who determines whether the existing weather or visibility is adequate for the approach and landing?
items.back: The pilot or aircraft operator.

items.front: Does a below-minima pilot report automatically prohibit all remaining approaches?
items.back: No. ATC adjusts the sequence and clearances as necessary.

items.front: How early should communications and control responsibility be transferred?
items.back: Early enough for the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches that limit.

items.front: Why must the transfer occur early?
items.back: So the receiving facility has time to issue onward clearance before the aircraft reaches the clearance limit.

items.front: What happens if the transfer is too late?
items.back: The receiving facility may not have time to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before it reaches it.

items.front: When must radio communications and control be transferred?
items.back: EARLY enough to allow the receiving facility to clear an aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it. Per 4-7-9.

items.front: To authorize a pilot to execute a particular instrument approach procedure, what must be specified?
items.back: The name of the approach as published on the approach chart.

items.front: If more than one procedure is published on a single chart and a specific procedure is to be flown, what must be specified?
items.back: The specific procedure to be flown.

items.front: If only one instrument approach of a particular type is published, what may be omitted from the identification?
items.back: The runway reference.

items.front: When an aircraft is cleared for an ILS or LDA approach, when must the glideslope-out advisory be issued?
items.back: At the time the approach clearance is issued, unless the procedure title already allows for that condition.

items.front: Where a STAR or ATS route connects to the IAP at an IAF or IF and the connection will be used, when is the aircraft cleared for the approach?
items.back: At least 3 NM prior to the IAF or IF, while specifying the connection fix.

items.front: May an aircraft be cleared direct to the FAF if that fix is not also an IAF?
items.back: No.

items.front: When a procedure turn or hold-in-lieu is depicted and the intercept angle is 90 degrees or less, what must ATC say if it does not want that maneuver flown?
items.back: The approach clearance must instruct the aircraft to conduct a straight-in approach.

items.front: For RNAV approaches containing RF legs, what direct clearance is prohibited?
items.back: Direct to any waypoint beginning or within an RF leg.

items.front: When GPS testing is actually occurring and a pilot requests or is cleared for an RNAV approach, what should ATC do?
items.back: Inform the pilot that GPS may not be available and request intentions.

items.front: What do the approach-clearance figures in 4-8-1 protect against?
items.back: Clearing an aircraft for an approach before it is properly positioned to begin at an IAF or IF, established on a published segment, or otherwise meeting the direct-to/intercept conditions.

items.front: When the pilot says they are unfamiliar with the procedure, what initial information must be specified?
items.back: The initial approach altitude.

items.front: What procedure-turn information must be specified to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: The direction and distance from the holding fix within which the procedure turn is to be completed, and the altitude at which the procedure turn is to be made.

items.front: What final-approach information must be specified to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: The final approach course and altitude.

items.front: When may missed approach procedures be specified for an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: If considered necessary.

items.front: What chart notations should be called out to an unfamiliar pilot?
items.back: Applicable approach-chart notations that place responsibility on the pilot to comply with a published limitation or instruction.

items.front: What phraseology introduces the unfamiliar-pilot approach briefing?
items.back: Initial approach at (altitude), procedure turn at (altitude), (number) minutes or miles (direction), final approach on (name of NAVAID) (specified) course, radial, or azimuth at (altitude).

items.front: If workload or traffic prevents a practice instrument approach, what should ATC tell the pilot?
items.back: The reason and, if applicable, the anticipated delay until the approach can be approved.

items.front: Should controller-imposed delays for practice approaches be indefinite?
items.back: No.

items.front: What general traffic-flow limitation applies to practice instrument approaches?
items.back: Except for military aircraft at military airfields, they must not disrupt the flow of other arriving and departing IFR or VFR aircraft.

items.front: What must a VFR aircraft practicing an instrument approach be told on initial contact or as soon as possible thereafter?
items.back: Maintain VFR.

items.front: What phraseology is used when a VFR practice approach is approved with no separation services provided?
items.back: Maintain VFR, practice approach approved, no separation services provided.

items.front: Is a VFR aircraft automatically authorized to execute the missed approach during a practice instrument approach?
items.back: No. It must be specifically requested by the pilot and approved by the controller.

items.front: When a facility directive requires IFR separation for a VFR practice approach, how long does that separation continue if the missed approach is approved?
items.back: Throughout the procedure, including the missed approach.

items.front: Before touchdown or crossing the landing threshold, how is an aircraft on a touch-and-go, low approach, or practice approach treated?
items.back: As an arriving aircraft.

items.front: After touchdown or threshold crossing, how is that aircraft treated?
items.back: As a departing aircraft.

items.front: When must departure instructions for a low approach or touch-and-go be issued?
items.back: Before the aircraft begins final descent.

items.front: What must climb-out instructions include after a low approach or touch-and-go?
items.back: A specific heading or a route of flight and altitude, except when the aircraft will maintain VFR and contact the tower.

items.front: What phraseology may be used for a low-approach climb-out to 6,000 and right heading 360?
items.back: After completing low approach, climb and maintain six thousand. Turn right, heading three six zero.

items.front: When may climb-out instructions be omitted after the first approach?
items.back: When the instructions remain the same.

items.front: What extra item must be stated in an approach clearance to an airport without an operating control tower or with a closed part-time tower?
items.back: The name of the airport.

items.front: What phraseology authorizes a specific approach to an uncontrolled airport?
items.back: Cleared (type) approach to (airport name).

items.front: What phraseology authorizes the pilot's choice of instrument approach to an uncontrolled airport?
items.back: Cleared approach to (airport name).

items.front: Why is the airport name included in the uncontrolled-airport approach clearance?
items.back: Because the location has no operating control tower, or the part-time tower is closed.

items.front: What must be stated in an approach clearance to an uncontrolled airport?
items.back: The name of the airport. Also applies when part-time towers are closed. Per 4-8-2.

items.front: When a terminal approach clearance is relayed through an authorized communication station, what must be included if it is required and available?
items.back: The weather report.

items.front: Which relay path is covered by this relayed-approach-clearance rule?
items.back: A communication station other than an air carrier company radio.

items.front: How may the controller satisfy the relayed-weather requirement through the station?
items.back: By telling the station to issue current weather.

items.front: May an air carrier company radio be treated as the equivalent authorized relay path for this rule?
items.back: No.

items.front: When may altitudes above those shown on a military high altitude instrument approach procedure be specified?
items.back: When they are required for separation.

items.front: Who coordinates the maximum altitudes that may be assigned on the military high altitude approach procedure?
items.back: The ATC facility and the military authority that originated the procedure.

items.front: Why are coordinated maximum assignable altitudes established for the procedure?
items.back: To prevent aircraft from exceeding descent-rate or airspeed limitations when above-charted altitudes are assigned.

items.front: If separation requires an altitude above the charted value on the military high altitude procedure, what still limits the assignment?
items.back: The coordinated maximum assignable altitude for that procedure.

items.front: When must the altitude shown in the approach procedure be specified in the approach clearance?
items.back: When adherence to that altitude is required for separation.

items.front: When may the controller omit specifying the altitude in the approach clearance?
items.back: When vertical separation will be provided by the pilot's adherence to the published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.

items.front: Which published-altitude types can support omission of the altitude from the approach clearance?
items.back: Published maximum, minimum, or mandatory altitudes.

items.front: If the published altitude is required for separation, may it be omitted because it is already on the chart?
items.back: No.

items.front: What chart family should be used when applying this altitude-specifying rule?
items.back: FAA or NGA instrument approach procedure charts appropriate for the aircraft executing the approach.

items.front: At what airports may circling approach instructions be given?
items.back: Only at airports with operational control towers.

items.front: If landing will be made on a runway other than the one aligned with the direction of instrument approach, what must be included in the clearance?
items.back: Instructions to circle to the runway in use.

items.front: When the direction of the circling maneuver is required, what must ATC state?
items.back: The direction using one of eight cardinal compass points and a left or right base or downwind leg as appropriate.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to direct a circling maneuver to a different runway?
items.back: Circle to Runway (number), or Circle (direction) of the airport or runway for a left or right base or downwind to Runway (number).

items.front: What kind of circling instruction must not be issued if it could take the aircraft outside the protected area?
items.back: An instruction such as "extend downwind leg."

items.front: If a published circling restriction conflicts with the controller's proposed maneuver, which one governs?
items.back: The published circling restriction governs; ATC should not issue contradictory instructions.

items.front: Where may circling approach instructions be given?
items.back: ONLY for aircraft landing at airports with operational control towers. Per 4-8-6(a).

items.front: When may a side-step maneuver be used?
items.back: When an instrument approach procedure authorizes clearing the aircraft to one runway while landing is made on a parallel runway.

items.front: What runway relationship defines the side-step maneuver?
items.back: The aircraft is cleared to one runway and lands on a parallel runway.

items.front: What phraseology may be used to authorize the side-step?
items.back: Cleared ILS Runway Seven Left approach. Side-step to Runway Seven Right.

items.front: What minima consideration applies to side-step maneuvers?
items.back: They require higher published weather minima or MDA.

items.front: Where are the higher side-step minima or MDA published?
items.back: On the instrument approach chart.

items.front: When should a change to the advisory service frequency be approved for an IFR aircraft landing at an airport not served by a tower or FSS?
items.back: When direct communications are no longer required.

items.front: What phraseology releases the aircraft to the local advisory frequency?
items.back: Change to advisory frequency approved.

items.front: What airport type does this communications-release rule address?
items.back: An airport not served by a control tower or FSS.

items.front: Why is an expeditious frequency change useful here?
items.back: It permits the aircraft to receive timely local airport traffic information.

items.front: When may an IFR aircraft change to advisory frequency?
items.back: When landing at an airport not served by tower or FSS and the controller no longer requires direct communications. Per 4-8-8.

items.front: Except for what type of aircraft does an approach clearance automatically authorize the depicted missed approach?
items.back: Except for a VFR aircraft practicing an instrument approach.

items.front: If an alternate missed approach is necessary, what must ATC issue?
items.back: The alternate missed approach description as published on the appropriate FAA or military form for the IAP.

items.front: When may the alternate missed approach description be omitted?
items.back: When the alternate missed approach is published and in effect via NOTAM.

items.front: After an aircraft commences the missed approach, where may it be vectored?
items.back: At or above the MVA or MIA, or under the applicable vectors-below-minimum-altitude provisions.

items.front: Unless otherwise cleared, when does the pilot begin a missed-approach turn?
items.back: At the missed approach point.

items.front: Does an approach clearance authorize a missed approach?
items.back: YES — except for VFR aircraft practicing instrument approaches. The approach clearance AUTOMATICALLY authorizes execution of the published missed approach procedure. Per 4-8-9(a).
